speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start by reviewing your vital signs from today's visit. Your systolic blood pressure is 128 mmHg.

speaker: patient
text: Thanks, Doctor. I’ve noticed some swelling in my ankles and lower legs over the past few days — it’s worse by evening.

speaker: doctor
text: I see. That’s important — we’ll assess that further. Let me also check your diastolic pressure… it’s 104 mmHg, which is elevated.

speaker: patient
text: Oh, really? I didn’t know it was that high. My usual readings at home have been around 130 over 80.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your symptoms, lab results, and clinical exam, the primary diagnosis is Hypothyroidism — we’ll confirm with TSH and free T4 shortly.

speaker: patient
text: That makes sense — I’ve been feeling fatigued, cold all the time, and my skin’s gotten quite dry.

speaker: doctor
text: Your hemoglobin today is 129 g/L — within normal range, though on the lower end for your age and sex.

speaker: doctor
text: I’m prescribing Lisinopril 20 mg once daily to help manage your hypertension and possibly reduce the edema.

speaker: patient
text: Okay, I’ll start that right away. Should I stop my current ACE inhibitor?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — discontinue your prior ACE inhibitor. Also, your fasting glucose has been trending up, so I’m adding Insulin glargine 16 units nightly to improve glycemic control.

speaker: patient
text: Got it. I’ve been checking my blood sugar more often — last night it was 152 mg/dL before bed.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate today is 59 bpm — slightly bradycardic, but not alarming yet. We’ll monitor closely given your new medications.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve felt a bit lightheaded when standing up quickly — could that be related?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly — orthostatic hypotension can occur with Lisinopril or thyroid hormone changes. Let’s recheck vitals after you sit quietly for two minutes.

speaker: doctor
text: Hmm — your heart rate dropped further to 50 bpm just now. That’s a notable change from earlier, so we’ll hold the Lisinopril dose for now and reassess tomorrow.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also noticed my energy hasn’t improved much since starting levothyroxine last month — could my dose need adjustment?

speaker: doctor
text: That’s likely — your hemoglobin has also declined to 119 g/L since our last draw, which may reflect worsening fatigue or early anemia of chronic disease.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure is now 142 mmHg — higher than the 128 we saw earlier. That, combined with the edema and bradycardia, suggests we need to revisit both your thyroid and cardiac management.

speaker: patient
text: Actually, when I checked my pulse this morning at home, it was 59 bpm — same as the reading you took first thing, before the drop.

speaker: doctor
text: Thank you for that clarification — that number helps us distinguish between baseline rhythm and acute changes. We’ll order a 12-lead ECG and repeat thyroid panel today.
